cards.front: What value does an uninitialized variable contain in C++?
cards.back: An indeterminate value (garbage value) - whatever data happened to be in that memory location previously. Reading it results in undefined behavior.

cards.text: {{c1::Undefined behavior}} (UB) is the result of executing code whose behavior is not well-defined by the C++ language.

cards.explanation:
Undefined behavior is one of the most dangerous problems in C++ because:
  • Can produce different results every time the program runs
  • May appear to work correctly most of the time
  • Can cause crashes, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities
  • Behavior can change with different compilers or optimization levels
  • Hard to debug because problems may not be reproducible
examples:
int x; // Uninitialized
std::cout << x; // UB: could print any value
May work in debug mode but crash in release
May work on one computer but fail on another

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Reading uninitialized variable x causes undefined behavior
cards.explanation: Variable x is defined but never initialized, so it contains a garbage value. Reading it invokes undefined behavior.

cards.front: What is the single best way to avoid undefined behavior related to uninitialized variables?
cards.back: Always initialize your variables upon creation. Use value initialization (empty braces) if you don't know what value to give yet.

cards.text: If you don't know what value to initialize a variable with, use {{c1::value initialization}} (empty braces): {{c2::int x{};}}

cards.front: Why should you make sure compiler warnings are enabled?
cards.back: Modern compilers can detect many cases where variables are used without being initialized and will warn you. These warnings can help catch bugs early.

cards.question: Which version is preferred?
cards.answer: Version 2 (initialization) is preferred over Version 1 (definition then assignment)
cards.explanation: Initialization is more efficient and safer than defining an uninitialized variable and then assigning to it. It also makes the intent clearer.

cards.front: What does it mean that undefined behavior is "ill-formed"?
cards.back: Ill-formed code violates the C++ language rules. The compiler is required to diagnose and error on ill-formed code. Undefined behavior, however, is NOT ill-formed - the compiler may not catch it.

cards.text: {{c1::Implementation-defined}} behavior means the behavior is left up to the implementation (compiler) to define, but the compiler must {{c2::document}} the behavior.

cards.concept: The relationship between variable definition location and initialization
cards.explanation:
Define variables as close to their first use as possible, and initialize them when defined:
  • Makes code easier to read (variable purpose is clear from context)
  • Reduces scope (limits where variable can be accessed)
  • Makes initialization more natural (value is often obvious from nearby code)
  • Prevents use-before-initialization bugs
examples:
// Good: defined near use
int x{calculateValue()};
